title: Fitting a dado rail
subtitle: Mark, cut, and fix a dado rail to a plastered wall
excerpt: How to fit a dado rail: finding the right height, marking a level line around the room, cutting internal and external mitres, fixing to a plastered wall, and filling nail holes for a clean painted finish.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Newey and Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery. City and Guilds Level 2 Painting and Decorating syllabus.
aliases: how to fit a dado rail

glossaryTerms.definition: A horizontal moulding fixed to a wall at approximately chair-back height (around 900 mm from the floor). Originally functional, protecting plaster from chair backs; now primarily decorative, dividing a wall visually into an upper and lower field.

A 45-degree cut at an inside corner that allows two lengths of moulding to meet without a gap. Both pieces are cut at 45 degrees on opposite sides so the faces of the moulding close together at the corner.

content.text: divides a room wall into two horizontal fields. The standard height is 900 mm from the floor, though rooms with high ceilings or low skirting sometimes suit 1,000 mm. The job requires accurate marking, clean mitre cuts at corners, and solid fixing to the plaster. A single room typically takes two to three hours of work.

Measure 900 mm up from the floor at one point on the wall and mark with a pencil. Use a spirit level to extend this mark as a continuous horizontal pencil line around all four walls of the room, checking the level as you go. On older houses where the floor is not flat, take the 900 mm measurement from the floor at several points and adjust the line slightly to maintain the visual horizontal rather than following the floor precisely.
Step 2, measure and cut the lengths
Measure each wall length and cut the rail to fit. On straight runs between two internal corners, a simple 90-degree square cut works. At internal corners, cut both pieces at 45 degrees to make an

. At external corners, both pieces are mitred the opposite way so the face of the moulding wraps around the corner. Use a mitre box or mitre saw set to 45 degrees; the cut must be clean and at the correct angle. Test-fit each piece before fixing.

Apply a bead of grab adhesive to the back of the rail, approximately 200 mm apart. Press the rail onto the pencil line, checking with the spirit level. On a solid plaster wall, nail through the rail into the wall at 600 mm centres using cut-floor brads or oval-wire nails (to avoid splitting the moulding), punched below the surface with a nail punch. On a plasterboard stud wall, locate the studs before starting and try to nail into them wherever possible, supplementing with adhesive in the spans between studs.

Fill nail holes with a fine interior filler, smooth flush, and allow to dry. Sand lightly with 120-grit paper. Fill any gaps at the corners or between the rail and the wall with a fine flexible decorator's caulk; tool smooth with a wet finger. The rail is now ready for painting.

attrs.symptom: Rail not sitting flush to the wall along its length
attrs.cause: The wall surface is uneven or bowed.
attrs.fix: Pack behind the rail with thin card or plywood at low spots to close the gap before nailing. Fill any remaining gap at the rail edge with decorator's caulk.

attrs.symptom: Internal mitre joint gaping at the face
attrs.cause: One or both cuts are not at exactly 45 degrees, or the wall is not a true right angle.
attrs.fix: Adjust the mitre angle slightly. Use a flexible filler or caulk in the joint; this is a common professional finish even on well-cut joins.
